questions.text: SONAR is mostly used by
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (D) Navigators**

**SONAR** stands for **Sound Navigation And Ranging**. It uses **ultrasonic waves** (high-frequency sound waves above 20,000 Hz) that are emitted underwater and the reflected echoes are used to detect and locate submerged objects such as submarines, underwater reefs, fish shoals, and to measure ocean depth.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Astronauts — INCORRECT:**
Astronauts operate in the vacuum of outer space. Sound cannot travel in vacuum, so SONAR is completely useless in space. Astronauts use **radio waves** for communication and navigation.

**(B) Doctors — INCORRECT:**
Doctors use **Ultrasonography (USG)** which also uses ultrasonic waves but for imaging internal body organs. The device is called a **sonograph/ultrasound machine**, NOT SONAR. SONAR is specifically a **navigation and detection** technology used in water.

**(C) Engineers — INCORRECT:**
Engineers may use various sound-based instruments for testing (like ultrasonic flaw detectors), but SONAR as a system is specifically designed for **underwater navigation and detection**, not general engineering work.

**(D) Navigators — CORRECT:**
Naval navigators and submarine operators use SONAR extensively to:
- Detect enemy submarines (active SONAR)
- Navigate underwater without visual reference
- Measure depth of ocean (echo sounding/fathometry)
- Locate underwater obstacles

**DRDO Relevance:** DRDO's Naval Systems & Materials laboratory develops advanced SONAR systems for Indian Navy submarines and ships. Questions on SONAR are frequently asked in DRDO CEPTAM exams to test applied physics knowledge.

questions.text: Which of the following is measured by 'Anemometer'?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) Force of the wind**

**Anemometer** is an instrument used to measure **wind speed (velocity/force of wind)** and sometimes wind direction. It is a key instrument in **meteorology** and **weather stations**.

**Working Principle:**
The most common type — the **cup anemometer** — has three or four hemispherical cups attached to horizontal arms on a vertical rod. Wind makes the cups rotate; the rate of rotation is proportional to wind speed. The faster the rotation, the higher the wind speed.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Velocity of water-flow — INCORRECT:**
Velocity of water flow is measured by a **Current Meter** or **Flow Meter**. In pipes, a **Venturimeter** or **Rotameter** is used. Anemometer is strictly for **air/wind** measurement.

**(B) Depth of water — INCORRECT:**
Depth of water (ocean/sea) is measured by a **Fathometer** or **Echo Sounder**, which uses ultrasonic waves. Depth of a well can be measured using a **weighted rope or echo technique**.

**(C) Force of the wind — CORRECT:**
Anemometer directly measures **wind speed**, which represents the force/velocity of wind. Wind speed is expressed in **km/h, m/s, or knots**. This is used in weather forecasting, aviation, and marine navigation.

**(D) Intensity of light — INCORRECT:**
Intensity of light is measured by a **Luxmeter** (illuminance) or **Photometer** (luminous intensity). Anemometer has no connection to light measurement.

**DRDO Relevance:** Defence applications include anemometers used at missile test ranges (like Pokhran, Chandipur) to measure wind speed before test launches, since wind affects projectile trajectory.

questions.text: The velocity of wind is measured by
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Anemometer**

**Anemometer** is the standard scientific instrument for measuring **wind velocity (speed)**.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Barometer — INCORRECT:**
A **Barometer** measures **atmospheric pressure** (in millibars or Pascals). It was invented by **Evangelista Torricelli** in 1643. A mercury barometer uses mercury column height to indicate air pressure. It does NOT measure wind speed.

**(B) Anemometer — CORRECT:**
**Anemometer** measures **wind speed/velocity**. Types include:
- **Cup Anemometer** — most common; rotation speed indicates wind speed
- **Vane Anemometer** — uses a propeller
- **Hot-wire Anemometer** — measures airflow speed electronically using heat loss from a thin wire
- **Ultrasonic Anemometer** — uses ultrasound pulses

Wind speed is reported in **Beaufort Scale** for weather forecasting.

**(C) Hydrometer — INCORRECT:**
A **Hydrometer** measures the **specific gravity (relative density) of liquids**. It is commonly used to check the density of battery acid, milk (lactometer is a type), alcohol, etc. It is NOT related to wind measurement.

**(D) Wind Vane — INCORRECT:**
A **Wind Vane (Weather Vane)** indicates the **direction** from which the wind is blowing (e.g., North, South, East, West). It does NOT measure the speed or velocity of wind. Together, anemometer + wind vane give complete wind data.

**DRDO Relevance:** Wind velocity measurement is critical in DRDO's **missile test range operations**, aerial target testing, and **atmospheric science research** conducted by DRDO labs like DMRL and ARDE.

questions.text:
Which one of the following is not correctly matched?
(A) Anemometer — Wind speed
(B) Ammeter — Electric current
(C) Tacheometer — Pressure difference
(D) Pyrometer — High temperature
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) Tacheometer — Pressure difference**

This is a **'NOT correctly matched'** question. Option (C) is the INCORRECT pairing.

**Analysis of each pair:**

**(A) Anemometer — Wind speed — CORRECTLY MATCHED:**
Anemometer accurately measures **wind speed/velocity**. This is a fundamental meteorological instrument. ✓

**(B) Ammeter — Electric current — CORRECTLY MATCHED:**
**Ammeter** measures **electric current** flowing in a circuit. It is always connected in **series** in the circuit. It has very **low resistance** so it doesn't disturb the circuit. ✓

**(C) Tacheometer — Pressure difference — INCORRECTLY MATCHED ✗ (This is the answer):**
A **Tacheometer** (or Tachymeter) is a **surveying instrument** used in civil engineering to measure **distances, angles, and elevations** rapidly (the word 'tachy' means swift). It is a type of **theodolite** used in land surveying.

The correct instrument for measuring **pressure difference** is a **Manometer** or **Differential Pressure Gauge**.
The instrument for measuring speed (of vehicles) is a **Tachometer/Speedometer**.
Hence, Tacheometer ≠ Pressure difference.

**(D) Pyrometer — High temperature — CORRECTLY MATCHED:**
**Pyrometer** is used to measure **very high temperatures** (above 1000°C) where conventional thermometers cannot be used. It works on the principle of **thermal radiation** emitted by hot bodies. ✓

**DRDO Relevance:** DRDO uses pyrometers extensively in **materials testing labs** (DMRL), rocket nozzle temperature measurement, and **armour material heat treatment** processes.

questions.text: Pyrometer is used to measure
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) High temperature**

**Pyrometer** is a **non-contact thermometer** used to measure **extremely high temperatures** (typically above 600°C to over 3000°C) by detecting the **thermal radiation** (infrared or visible light) emitted by the hot body.

**Principle:** Based on **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** — the amount of radiation emitted by a body is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature (T⁴). By measuring the intensity of emitted radiation, the temperature is calculated.

**Types of Pyrometers:**
- **Optical/Disappearing Filament Pyrometer:** Compares the brightness of a standard lamp filament with that of the hot body.
- **Total Radiation Pyrometer:** Measures total radiation emitted (infrared + visible).
- **Infrared Pyrometer:** Detects IR radiation only.

**Why each option is wrong:**

**(A) Air pressure — INCORRECT:**
Air pressure is measured by **Barometer** (atmospheric) or **Manometer** (gas pressure in containers).

**(B) Humidity — INCORRECT:**
Humidity (moisture content in air) is measured by **Hygrometer**. Relative humidity is measured using **wet and dry bulb hygrometer** (psychrometer).

**(C) High temperature — CORRECT:**
Pyrometer is specifically designed for high-temperature measurement where mercury or gas thermometers fail (they can measure only up to ~600°C).

**(D) Density — INCORRECT:**
Density of liquids is measured by **Hydrometer**. Density of solids can be determined by **Archimedes' principle** using a balance.

**DRDO Relevance:** Pyrometers are used in DRDO's propellant testing, rocket engine combustion chamber temperature measurement, and metallurgical research at DMRL, Hyderabad.

questions.text: Which one of the following devices is used to measure extremely high temperature?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) Pyrometer**

**Pyrometer** is the only device among the options specifically designed to measure **extremely high temperatures** non-contactually.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Pyrometer — CORRECT:**
Measures **extremely high temperatures** (600°C–3500°C+) using the principle of **thermal radiation**. It is essential in metallurgy, ceramics, and aerospace industries where direct contact measurement is impossible.

**Key fact:** The word 'Pyro' comes from Greek meaning **fire**, hence pyrometer = fire measurer.

**(B) Photometer — INCORRECT:**
A **Photometer** measures the **intensity (luminous intensity) of light**. It is used in photography, lighting design, and optics. It has NO relation to temperature measurement.

**(C) Phonometer — INCORRECT:**
A **Phonometer** measures the **intensity/power of sound** or the force of the human voice. 'Phono' means sound in Greek. It is used in acoustics. Not related to temperature.

**(D) Pycnometer — INCORRECT:**
A **Pycnometer** (also spelled Pyknometer) is a laboratory glassware used to determine the **precise density (specific gravity) of liquids and solids**. It works by comparing weights. Not related to temperature measurement at all.

**Memory Trick for DRDO exam:**
- **Pyro**meter → Fire/High Temperature
- **Photo**meter → Light intensity
- **Phono**meter → Sound
- **Pycno**meter → Density

**DRDO Relevance:** In DRDO labs like ASL (Advanced Systems Laboratory), pyrometers are used to measure temperatures during hypersonic vehicle re-entry simulation and rocket nozzle testing.

questions.text: Which one of the following thermometers is known as pyrometer?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Radiation thermometers**

**Pyrometers are classified as Radiation Thermometers** because they measure temperature based on the **electromagnetic radiation** (mainly infrared and visible light) emitted by the hot body — without any physical contact.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Thermo-electric thermometers — INCORRECT:**
Thermo-electric thermometers (Thermocouples) work on the **Seebeck Effect** — when two dissimilar metals are joined at two junctions and there is a temperature difference between the junctions, an **EMF (electromotive force) is generated**. They can measure high temperatures but are contact-based and are NOT called pyrometers.

**(B) Radiation thermometers — CORRECT:**
Pyrometers are **radiation thermometers**. They work on the principle that every body above absolute zero emits thermal radiation. The wavelength and intensity of radiation are directly related to the body's temperature (via **Planck's law** and **Wien's displacement law**).
- At lower temperatures → infrared radiation
- At very high temperatures → visible light (glowing red, orange, white hot)

Pyrometers detect this radiation and convert it to temperature readings. Hence they are called **radiation thermometers**.

**(C) Gas thermometers — INCORRECT:**
Gas thermometers use the **expansion of gas** (usually hydrogen or helium) with temperature to measure it. They are highly accurate and used as standard thermometers but are contact-based and cannot measure ultra-high temperatures. They are NOT pyrometers.

**(D) Liquid thermometers — INCORRECT:**
Liquid thermometers (like mercury or alcohol thermometers) use **thermal expansion of liquids**. Mercury thermometers work up to ~360°C. They are contact-based and NOT pyrometers.

**DRDO Relevance:** Understanding radiation thermometry is crucial for DRDO scientists working in **infrared sensor development, thermal imaging systems, and missile seeker design** — all of which rely on detecting IR radiation emitted by targets.

questions.text: The thermometer which is used to measure 2000°C temperature is
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) Total radiation pyrometer**

**Only a Total Radiation Pyrometer** can measure temperatures as high as **2000°C** accurately.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Gas thermometer — INCORRECT:**
Gas thermometers typically operate in the range of **-270°C to about 500°C**. At 2000°C, the gas container would melt or rupture. They cannot be used for such extreme temperatures.

**(B) Mercury thermometer — INCORRECT:**
Mercury has a **boiling point of 357°C** and melts its glass casing well before 2000°C. Standard mercury thermometers work only from **-39°C to about 360°C**. Completely unsuitable for 2000°C measurement.

**(C) Total radiation pyrometer — CORRECT:**
The **Total Radiation Pyrometer** (also called **Stefan-Boltzmann Pyrometer**) collects all the radiation (infrared + visible + UV) emitted by a hot body and focuses it onto a **thermopile or bolometer** sensor. Since it is **non-contact**, it can measure temperatures from:
- 700°C to over 3500°C

This makes it ideal for measuring temperatures of:
- Molten metals (steel: ~1500°C, tungsten: ~3400°C)
- Blast furnaces (~2000°C)
- Rocket engine exhausts
- Electric arc furnaces

**(D) Steam pressure thermometer — INCORRECT:**
Steam pressure thermometers work by measuring the **vapour pressure of a liquid** (usually a volatile liquid in a bulb). Their range is limited (approximately **-40°C to 350°C**). Far below the requirement of 2000°C.

**DRDO Relevance:** DRDO's **DMRL (Defence Metallurgical Research Laboratory)** uses radiation pyrometers for measuring temperatures during processing of **special steels, titanium alloys, and ceramic composites** used in military hardware.

questions.text: 'Pyrheliometer' is used for measuring
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Solar radiation**

**Pyrheliometer** is a precision instrument used to measure the **direct beam solar irradiance** (direct solar radiation) falling on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays.

**Etymology:**
- **Pyr** = Fire/Heat (Greek)
- **Helio** = Sun (Greek)
- **Meter** = Measure
So, Pyrheliometer literally means **'Sun-heat measurer'**.

**Working:** It consists of a narrow tube pointing directly at the Sun, with a radiation-absorbing sensor (thermopile) at the bottom. It measures solar irradiance in **W/m²**.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Sun spots — INCORRECT:**
Sunspots are dark areas on the Sun's surface caused by intense magnetic activity. They are observed using **solar telescopes** with special filters (like hydrogen-alpha filters) — NOT a pyrheliometer.

**(B) Solar radiation — CORRECT:**
Pyrheliometer measures **direct solar radiation** (beam irradiance). The **solar constant** (~1361 W/m²) is measured using pyrheliometers aboard satellites and at ground stations.

**(C) Air temperature — INCORRECT:**
Air temperature is measured using standard **thermometers** or **thermistors**. Pyrheliometer measures energy from the Sun, not ambient air temperature.

**(D) Temperature of plants — INCORRECT:**
Leaf/plant surface temperature can be measured using **infrared thermometers or thermocouples**. Pyrheliometer is not designed for this purpose.

**Important distinction:**
- **Pyrheliometer** → measures DIRECT solar beam radiation
- **Pyranometer** → measures TOTAL (direct + diffuse) solar radiation on a horizontal surface
- **Pyrgeometer** → measures terrestrial/atmospheric infrared radiation

**DRDO Relevance:** Understanding solar radiation measurement is important for DRDO's **Solar Energy Research, satellite thermal design, and directed-energy weapon** systems that rely on solar tracking technology.

questions.text: What is measured by manometer?
options: Pressure of oil on the surface
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Pressure of gas**

**Manometer** is an instrument used to measure the **pressure of gases** (enclosed gas or gas in pipes/vessels).

**Types of Manometers:**
1. **U-tube Manometer:** A U-shaped tube partially filled with liquid (mercury or water). The difference in liquid levels in the two arms gives the gauge pressure of the gas.
2. **Bourdon Tube Manometer:** Uses an elastic curved tube that straightens under pressure — used in pressure gauges.
3. **Digital Manometer:** Electronic pressure sensors with digital display.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Air pressure (atmospheric) — INCORRECT:**
Atmospheric pressure is measured by a **Barometer** (Torricelli's barometer or aneroid barometer). While air is a gas, 'atmospheric pressure' specifically refers to the ambient air pressure measured by a barometer, not a manometer. Manometer measures the pressure of **enclosed gases** relative to atmospheric pressure.

**(B) Pressure of gas — CORRECT:**
Manometer measures **gauge pressure** (pressure relative to atmosphere) or **absolute pressure** of gases in containers, pipelines, engines, etc. For example, it is used to measure:
- Pressure in gas cylinders
- Blood pressure (sphygmomanometer is a specialized manometer)
- Pressure in steam pipes

**(C) Density of liquids — INCORRECT:**
Density of liquids is measured by a **Hydrometer** (relative density) or calculated from mass and volume measurements.

**(D) Pressure of oil on the surface — INCORRECT:**
While oil pressure in an engine can be measured by a manometer, the 'pressure of oil on the surface' implies surface pressure which is more related to **Pascal's principle**. The specific instrument for oil pressure in automotive context is an **oil pressure gauge**. The most accurate general answer for manometer is pressure of gas.

**DRDO Relevance:** Manometers are used in DRDO's **TBRL (Terminal Ballistics Research Laboratory)** and **ARDE (Armament Research & Development Establishment)** to measure gas pressures in gun barrels and propellant chambers during weapons testing.

questions.text:
Which one of the following pair is not correctly matched?
(A) Odometer — Measuring instrument for distance covered by motor wheels
(B) Ondometer — Measuring instrument for frequency of electromagnetic waves
(C) Audiometer — Device for measuring sound intensity
(D) Ammeter — Measuring instrument for electric power
options: Odometer — Measuring instrument for distance covered by motor wheels
Ondometer — Measuring instrument for frequency of electromagnetic waves
Audiometer — Device for measuring sound intensity
Ammeter — Measuring instrument for electric power
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (D) Ammeter — Measuring instrument for electric power**

This is a **'NOT correctly matched'** question. Option (D) contains the INCORRECT pairing.

**Analysis of each pair:**

**(A) Odometer — Distance covered by motor wheels — CORRECTLY MATCHED ✓:**
An **Odometer** (also called hodometer) is the instrument in vehicles that measures the **total distance travelled** by the vehicle. It is integrated into the vehicle's speedometer. Derived from Greek: 'hodos' = path, 'metron' = measure.

**(B) Ondometer — Frequency of electromagnetic waves — CORRECTLY MATCHED ✓:**
An **Ondometer** (Wave meter) measures the **frequency or wavelength of electromagnetic waves**, especially radio waves. Used in telecommunications and signal analysis.

**(C) Audiometer — Sound intensity — CORRECTLY MATCHED ✓:**
**Audiometer** is a device used to measure a person's hearing ability (sound intensity threshold). It generates sounds of various frequencies and intensities to test **hearing acuity**. Used in ENT (Ear, Nose, Throat) clinics.

**(D) Ammeter — Electric power — INCORRECTLY MATCHED ✗ (This is the answer):**
**Ammeter** measures **electric current** (in Amperes), NOT electric power.

**Electric power** (in Watts) is measured by a **Wattmeter**.

**Important distinction:**
- **Ammeter** → Electric current (A)
- **Voltmeter** → Electric voltage (V)
- **Wattmeter** → Electric power (W)
- **Ohmmeter** → Electrical resistance (Ω)

Ammeter is connected in **series**; has very **low resistance**. If connected to measure power, it would give wrong values.

**DRDO Relevance:** Accurate knowledge of electrical measuring instruments is tested in DRDO CEPTAM for technical posts in electronics and electrical engineering streams.

questions.text: Which instrument is used to measure atmospheric pressure?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Barometer**

**Barometer** is the standard instrument to measure **atmospheric pressure (air pressure)**.

**History:** Invented by **Evangelista Torricelli** in **1643**. Standard atmospheric pressure = **101,325 Pa = 1 atm = 760 mmHg = 1013.25 mbar**.

**Types of Barometers:**
1. **Mercury Barometer (Torricelli's Barometer):** A glass tube filled with mercury inverted in a mercury trough. The height of mercury column (~760 mm) represents atmospheric pressure.
2. **Aneroid Barometer:** Uses a sealed, flexible metallic chamber (aneroid capsule) that expands/contracts with pressure changes. Portable and used in aircraft altimeters.
3. **Digital Barometer:** Uses electronic pressure sensors (MEMS technology).

**Why each option is wrong:**

**(A) Hydrometer — INCORRECT:**
**Hydrometer** measures the **specific gravity (relative density) of liquids** — used for checking battery acid, alcohol content, and milk density. NOT for atmospheric pressure.

**(B) Barometer — CORRECT:**
Measures **atmospheric pressure**. A sudden **fall in barometer reading** indicates approaching storm/rain. A gradual **rise** indicates fair weather.

**(C) Manometer — INCORRECT:**
**Manometer** measures the **pressure of enclosed gases** in pipes or containers. It measures gauge pressure (relative to atmosphere), NOT the atmosphere itself.

**(D) Hygrometer — INCORRECT:**
**Hygrometer** measures **humidity (moisture content)** in the atmosphere, NOT pressure.

**DRDO Relevance:** Barometers are essential in DRDO's **atmospheric research, missile trajectory calculations, and ballistic testing** where atmospheric pressure directly affects projectile behaviour and aerodynamics.

questions.text: The liquid/liquids used in the simple barometer is/are
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Mercury**

In a **simple (Torricellian) barometer**, only **Mercury** is used as the liquid.

**Why Mercury is used in a simple barometer:**
1. **High density (13,600 kg/m³):** Mercury's high density means a column of only **~760 mm (76 cm)** is needed to balance atmospheric pressure. If water were used, the column would need to be **~10.3 metres** — impractically tall.
2. **Does not evaporate easily:** Mercury has very **low vapour pressure**, so the vacuum (Torricellian vacuum) above the mercury column is nearly perfect.
3. **Does not wet glass:** Mercury forms a **convex meniscus** and doesn't stick to glass, giving clear readings.
4. **Opaque:** Easily visible for reading.

**Why other liquids are NOT used in a SIMPLE barometer:**

**(A) Water — INCORRECT for simple barometer:**
Water has low density (1000 kg/m³). A water barometer would need a column **~10.3 m tall** — completely impractical for a handheld or lab instrument. Also, water evaporates and forms vapour pressure that would invalidate the vacuum.

**(C) Alcohol — INCORRECT for simple barometer:**
Alcohol (ethanol) has even lower density (~790 kg/m³) than water, requiring a ~13 m tall column. Also has significant vapour pressure.

**(D) All of these — INCORRECT:**
Only mercury is used in the **simple/standard barometer**. Water and alcohol barometers exist in special forms but are NOT the standard simple barometer.

**DRDO Relevance:** Understanding barometric principles is important for DRDO's **meteorological instruments division** and ballistic trajectory calculations in weapons systems.

questions.text: The density of milk can be obtained by the use of
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) Lactometer**

**Lactometer** is a specialized hydrometer used specifically to measure the **density (relative density/specific gravity) of milk** to determine its **purity**.

**How Lactometer Works:**
Pure milk has a specific gravity of approximately **1.026–1.032** (at 15°C). When milk is **adulterated with water**, its density decreases. A lactometer floats higher in pure milk and lower in watered-down milk.

**Why each option is right or wrong:**

**(A) Hydrometer — INCORRECT (as the BEST answer):**
A **Hydrometer** measures the **specific gravity of liquids in general**. While technically a lactometer IS a type of hydrometer, a lactometer is specifically calibrated for milk. The question asks specifically about milk density — lactometer is the precise and correct answer.

**(B) Butyrometer — INCORRECT:**
A **Butyrometer** (Gerber Butyrometer) is used to measure the **fat content (butterfat percentage) of milk and dairy products**. It measures fat, NOT the overall density or purity of milk.

**(C) Lactometer — CORRECT:**
Specifically designed and calibrated to measure **milk density**. Used by food inspectors, dairy companies, and FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) to check milk adulteration.

**(D) Thermometer — INCORRECT:**
A thermometer measures **temperature**. While temperature affects milk quality, a thermometer cannot measure milk density.

**DRDO Relevance:** While not directly related to defence, understanding specific scientific instruments is a standard requirement in DRDO CEPTAM General Science section. Knowledge of specialised hydrometers (lactometer, saccharometer, alcoholometer) is often tested.

questions.text: Which instrument is used for measuring humidity in the air?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Hygrometer**

**Hygrometer** is the instrument used to measure **humidity** (the amount of water vapour/moisture) present in the **air or other gases**.

**Types of Hygrometers:**
1. **Sling Psychrometer / Wet-and-Dry Bulb Hygrometer:** Two thermometers — one with a wet wick. Evaporation from the wet bulb causes cooling. The difference in readings gives relative humidity (using psychrometric tables).
2. **Hair Hygrometer:** Human or animal hair expands/contracts with humidity. Hair length change indicates RH.
3. **Capacitive Hygrometer:** Uses a hygroscopic material whose dielectric constant changes with humidity. Used in digital weather stations.
4. **Dew Point Hygrometer:** Measures the temperature at which dew forms, from which humidity is calculated.

**Why each option is wrong:**

**(A) Hydrometer — INCORRECT:**
**Hydrometer** measures the **specific gravity of liquids** — NOT humidity. Common confusion due to similar names (hydro = water).

**(B) Hygrometer — CORRECT:**
Measures **relative humidity (%)** — the ratio of actual water vapour present to the maximum water vapour the air can hold at that temperature.

**(C) Spectrometer — INCORRECT:**
**Spectrometer** is used to measure the **spectrum of light** (wavelength, frequency, intensity). Used in spectroscopic analysis of chemicals and materials. Unrelated to humidity.

**(D) Eudiometer — INCORRECT:**
**Eudiometer** is a graduated glass tube used to measure the **volume change of gases** during chemical reactions (especially combustion). Used to measure oxygen in air. NOT for humidity.

**DRDO Relevance:** Humidity measurement is critical in DRDO labs for **explosive storage conditions** (humidity affects propellant stability), **electronics component testing**, and **environmental chambers** used in equipment qualification testing.

questions.text: Hygrometer is used to measure
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) Humidity in atmosphere**

**Hygrometer** is exclusively used to measure **humidity (moisture content/water vapour)** in the atmosphere.

**Key Concepts:**
- **Absolute Humidity:** Mass of water vapour per unit volume of air (g/m³)
- **Relative Humidity (RH):** Ratio of actual vapour pressure to saturation vapour pressure, expressed as a percentage (%)
- **Specific Humidity:** Mass of water vapour per unit mass of moist air (g/kg)

**Why each option is wrong:**

**(A) Humidity in atmosphere — CORRECT:**
This is the primary and only function of a hygrometer.

**(B) Atmospheric pressure — INCORRECT:**
Atmospheric pressure is measured by **Barometer**. Confusion may arise since both measure atmospheric properties, but they measure completely different quantities.

**(C) High temperature — INCORRECT:**
High temperature is measured by **Pyrometer** (for very high temperatures) or **Thermometer** (for moderate temperatures). Hygrometer is NOT a temperature-measuring device.

**(D) Velocity of wind — INCORRECT:**
Velocity/speed of wind is measured by **Anemometer**. Hygrometer has no mechanism to detect wind speed.

**Standard values to remember for DRDO exam:**
- Comfortable indoor humidity: **40–60% RH**
- Safe humidity for explosive storage: Strict limits maintained
- India average monsoon humidity: Can reach **85–95% RH**
- Desert areas: As low as **10–20% RH**

**DRDO Relevance:** Humidity control is essential in DRDO labs for **electronic warfare equipment storage, propellant manufacturing, corrosion prevention in weapons systems, and calibration laboratory environments**.

questions.text: Which of the following is correctly matched?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) Thermoresistor — Electronic Thermometer**

**Analysis of each pair:**

**(A) Thermoresistor — Electronic Thermometer — CORRECTLY MATCHED ✓:**
A **Thermoresistor** (Thermistor = **THERMally sensitive resISTOR**) is a type of resistor whose **electrical resistance changes significantly with temperature**. There are two types:
- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **decreases** as temperature increases. Used in temperature measurement, inrush current limiters.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **increases** as temperature increases. Used in self-regulating heaters, motor protection.

Thermistors are the core sensing element in **electronic thermometers** (digital clinical thermometers). Hence **Thermoresistor = Electronic Thermometer** is correctly matched.

**(B) Capacitor — Thermometer — INCORRECTLY MATCHED ✗:**
A **Capacitor** stores electrical charge (energy) and is used in filtering, timing circuits, oscillators, and power factor correction — NOT as a thermometer. Temperature measurement using capacitance is niche and not standard.

**(C) Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) — Rectifier — INCORRECTLY MATCHED ✗:**
A **BJT** is used as an **amplifier** or **switch** (NOT a rectifier). A **rectifier** converts AC to DC and uses **diodes** (specifically PN junction diodes). A BJT can be made to rectify but is not its primary/standard use.

**(D) Junction Diode — Amplifier — INCORRECTLY MATCHED ✗:**
A **Junction Diode** (PN diode) is primarily used as a **rectifier** (converting AC to DC), NOT an amplifier. **Amplification** is done by **transistors (BJT, FET)** or **operational amplifiers**.

**DRDO Relevance:** Semiconductor devices (thermistors, transistors, diodes) are fundamental to DRDO's **electronic warfare, radar systems, missile guidance electronics, and sensor development**.

questions.text: The name of the equipment used for measuring blood pressure is
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Sphygmomanometer**

**Sphygmomanometer** is the medical instrument used to measure **blood pressure** (arterial blood pressure).

**Etymology:**
- **Sphygmo** = Pulse (Greek)
- **Mano** = Pressure (Greek)
- **Meter** = Measure

**How it works:**
A **cuff** is wrapped around the upper arm and inflated to compress the brachial artery, stopping blood flow. Air is slowly released while listening with a **stethoscope**:
- First sound heard (Korotkoff sound) = **Systolic pressure** (when heart contracts, ~120 mmHg normal)
- Last sound = **Diastolic pressure** (when heart relaxes, ~80 mmHg normal)

**Normal BP:** 120/80 mmHg
**Hypertension (high BP):** >140/90 mmHg
**Hypotension (low BP):** <90/60 mmHg

**Why each option is wrong:**

**(A) Teachometer — INCORRECT:**
There is no standard instrument called 'Teachometer.' This is a distractor. A **Tachometer** measures rotational speed (RPM) of an engine.

**(B) Sphygmomanometer — CORRECT:**
The ONLY standard instrument for measuring blood pressure.

**(C) Actiometer — INCORRECT:**
An **Actinometer** measures the **heating power of solar radiation** (radiant energy flux). Not related to blood pressure.

**(D) Barometer — INCORRECT:**
A **Barometer** measures **atmospheric pressure**, not blood pressure. Though both measure pressure, they are entirely different instruments. Blood pressure is measured in **mmHg** while atmospheric pressure is in **mbar or Pa**.

**DRDO Relevance:** DRDO's **INMAS (Institute of Nuclear Medicine and Allied Sciences)** and **DIBER (Defence Institute of Bio-Energy Research)** study soldier physiology. Blood pressure measurement instruments are used in soldier fitness evaluation.

questions.text: Which one of the following can be used to measure temperature above 1500°C?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (D) Pyrometer**

For temperatures **above 1500°C**, only a **Pyrometer** (radiation-based thermometer) is suitable.

**Temperature ranges of different thermometers:**

**(A) Clinical thermometer — INCORRECT:**
Clinical thermometer (mercury-in-glass) measures **body temperature** in a very narrow range of **35°C to 42°C**. It is designed only for human/animal body temperature measurement. Completely useless above 100°C.

**(B) Thermoelectric couple thermometer (Thermocouple) — INCORRECT (as the best answer):**
Thermocouples work on the **Seebeck Effect** — two different metals joined at junctions produce voltage proportional to temperature difference. Common types:
- **Type K (Chromel-Alumel):** Up to ~1260°C
- **Type S (Platinum-Rhodium):** Up to ~1480°C
- **Type B:** Up to ~1820°C

While **high-end** thermocouples can measure up to ~1800°C, they are contact-based and degrade at very high temperatures. Above 1500°C is the **boundary of thermocouple reliability**.

**(C) Platinum resistance thermometer — INCORRECT:**
Platinum resistance thermometers (PRTs) use **change in electrical resistance** of platinum with temperature. Range: **-200°C to 850°C** (standard) to ~1000°C (special types). Far below 1500°C.

**(D) Pyrometer — CORRECT:**
Pyrometers are **non-contact radiation thermometers** that can measure temperatures from ~600°C to over **3500°C** accurately. Types:
- Optical pyrometer: 700°C–3500°C
- Total radiation pyrometer: Up to 3000°C+
- Infrared pyrometer: Wide range

No physical contact is needed, making them ideal for molten metals, furnaces, and rocket testing.

**DRDO Relevance:** Temperature measurement above 1500°C is critical in DRDO for **rocket nozzle materials testing, jet engine development, and explosive characterisation** at DRDL and ARDE.

questions.text: The apparatus used to measure the intensity of light is known as
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) Luxmeter**

**Luxmeter** (also called **Lux meter** or **Light meter** or **Illuminance meter**) is the instrument used to measure the **intensity (illuminance) of light** in the unit of **Lux (lx)**.

**How it works:** A **photodetector (photovoltaic cell/photodiode)** converts incident light into an electrical signal proportional to light intensity. The reading is displayed in Lux.

**Important light measurement units:**
- **Lux (lx):** Illuminance = Luminous flux per unit area (lm/m²) — measured by Luxmeter
- **Candela (cd):** Luminous intensity — measured by Photometer
- **Lumen (lm):** Luminous flux — total light output of a source

**Why each option is wrong:**

**(A) Anemometer — INCORRECT:**
**Anemometer** measures **wind speed/velocity**. It is a meteorological instrument with no relation to light measurement.

**(B) Calorimeter — INCORRECT:**
**Calorimeter** is used to measure the **heat energy released or absorbed** during chemical reactions, combustion, or phase changes. For example, **Bomb Calorimeter** measures calorific value of fuels. Not related to light intensity.

**(C) Luxmeter — CORRECT:**
Measures **illuminance (light intensity per unit area)** in Lux. Widely used in:
- Photography and cinematography
- Workplace lighting standards (OSHA/BIS standards)
- Optical quality testing
- Agriculture (plant light requirements)

**(D) Altimeter — INCORRECT:**
**Altimeter** measures **altitude (height above sea level)** using atmospheric pressure changes (aneroid barometer principle) or radar (in aircraft). Used in aviation, mountaineering, and missiles. Not related to light.

**DRDO Relevance:** Luxmeters are used in DRDO's **IRDE (Instruments Research and Development Establishment)** for calibrating optical instruments, night vision devices, and testing **electro-optical systems** for defence applications.
